blurb: Named for Boulder itself — a thornless, arching native shrub hung with big white rose-like flowers in late spring over handsome maple-shaped leaves; tough, shade-tolerant, and a foothills classic.
water: Low to moderate; xeric once established
seasons: Large white flowers late spring–early summer; maple-like leaves turning yellow in fall; dry reddish fruit
wildlife: Native bees; fruit and cover for birds

care.hardiness: A Front Range native of the rocky foothills, fully hardy through Boulder's zone 5b–6a and well adapted to our high-altitude sun and cold.[1][2]
care.planting: Plant in spring after the mid-May last frost or in early fall into well-drained ground; it transplants best as young container stock. Containers dry fast and their roots are less cold-hardy, so keep potted plants watered and shelter them over winter.[1][2]
care.sun: Full sun to part shade; in the wild it grows on open rocky slopes and in light woodland edges of the foothills.[1][2]
care.soil: Needs well-drained soil but tolerates a wide pH range from acid to neutral to the basic, alkaline clay common around Boulder.[2]
care.water: Drought tolerant once established as a foothills native; give occasional deep water the first year, then little supplemental water in our semi-arid climate.[1][2]
care.propagation: Grows from seed (cold-moist stratified) and, being a thornless bramble, also spreads and roots from suckers and tip layers, the easiest way to make new plants.[1][2]
care.bloom: Large, bright-white five-petaled flowers up to ~2.5 in across appear in late spring on the previous year's canes, set off by maple-like lobed leaves on a thornless, arching shrub 4–5 ft tall (occasionally to ~9 ft).[1][2]
care.maintenance: Low maintenance; thin or remove the oldest spent canes after fruiting to keep it open, much as you would any cane fruit.[1][2]
care.harvest: Dark-red raspberry-like fruit ripens in summer, but despite the name 'deliciosus' it is typically dry, seedy, and bland — grow this shrub for its showy flowers and habit rather than a crop.[1][2]

references.name: USDA Forest Service — Delicious Raspberry (Rubus deliciosus), Plant of the Week
Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Rubus deliciosus

edible.card: Edible fruit, but dry, seedy & bland
edible.summary: The dark-red fruit is edible raw or cooked but — despite the name 'deliciosus' — is usually dry, seedy, and insipid with little flesh.[1][2]
edible.parts: Only the ripe red aggregate fruit is eaten; it can be taken raw or cooked, though most foragers find it disappointing.[1][2]
edible.caution: The fruit is harmless but typically dry and bland with lots of seed and little pulp, so it's not highly valued as food; quality varies plant to plant. Eat only fully ripe fruit.[1][2]
